question: A pharmaceutical company has 12 drug candidates in its pipeline across different stages. It has $500M to allocate over 3 years. Each drug has a probability of advancing to the next stage, estimated NPV if successful, and a cost to advance. Drugs compete for the same chemist teams (max 4 drugs in Phase 2 simultaneously, max 2 in Phase 3). You must decide which drugs to advance, shelve, or out-license. The constraint is: every drug shelved has a 40% chance a competitor develops it and gains market advantage in that indication. Design a decision framework that balances portfolio risk, resource constraints, team constraints, and competitive dynamics — and explain why standard NPV-maximization fails here.
rubric: Why NPV-maximization fails: (1) Ignores resource constraints (team capacity) which creates interdependencies between drug choices. (2) Ignores competitive dynamics — shelving a drug doesn't eliminate its strategic value, it creates competitive risk. (3) Ignores portfolio correlation — drugs in same indication category have correlated failure modes. (4) Ignores optionality value of drugs that could be out-licensed.

Framework components: (a) Risk-adjusted NPV per drug (standard). (b) Resource utilization score — which drugs efficiently use the bottleneck (chemist teams). (c) Competitive threat score — how bad is it if a competitor gets this? (d) Strategic optionality — licensing value even if not internally developed. (e) Portfolio diversification — correlated vs independent bets.
Constraint-first approach: Phase 3 can hold max 2 drugs. Select the 2 highest rNPV drugs that fit Phase 3 first. Then fill Phase 2 with 4 drugs that maximize diversified rNPV given remaining budget.

Shelving vs. out-licensing decision: for each drug not internally advanced, calculate: 40% * (competitor's expected profit impact to us) vs. out-licensing revenue. License any drug where licensing value > competitive threat cost.
Portfolio diversification: avoid selecting 4 Phase 2 drugs all targeting the same indication (correlated failure risk). Use a correlation matrix of drug mechanisms to construct orthogonal bets.

Dynamic allocation: reserve 20% of budget ($100M) as a contingency fund — Phase 2 failures free up team slots and budget for acceleration of remaining drugs. Static allocation is suboptimal in a sequential decision problem.

Real options framing: Phase 1 and 2 drugs are options, not just costs. The option value of keeping a drug alive (even at high cost) includes the ability to exercise it later when market conditions improve.

question: An AI company has built a system that can automate 40% of legal work, reducing law firm costs by $80B/year globally. However, deployment would displace 300,000 lawyers over 10 years. The technology is also dual-use: the same system can identify legal loopholes to help corporations avoid $200B/year in taxes, undermining government revenues. Three deployment strategies: (A) Unrestricted commercial release, (B) Release only to public defenders and legal aid organizations (access-equity focus), (C) License to governments and large firms with usage monitoring. Model the 10-year net social welfare of each strategy across dimensions of: legal access equity, economic efficiency, government revenue, employment, and innovation. Which strategy maximizes social welfare and under what value system?
rubric: Strategy A (unrestricted): Legal cost reduction $80B/year (efficiency gain). Tax avoidance exploitation: -$200B/year in government revenue. Net economic: -$120B/year. Employment: -300K jobs. Access equity: improved for those who can afford the tool. Over 10 years, government revenue loss dominates.

Strategy B (public defenders only): Legal access equity: dramatically improved (most underserved get AI legal help). Economic efficiency: minimal (small fraction of legal market). Tax avoidance: zero (public defenders don't do tax law). Employment: minimal displacement. Government revenue: neutral. Best on equity, worst on efficiency.

Strategy C (licensed with monitoring): Cost reduction: $40-60B/year (partial adoption). Tax avoidance: monitorable and partially preventable. Employment: -150K jobs (slower). Government revenue: -$50-100B/year (partially mitigated by monitoring).

Under utilitarian welfare (maximize aggregate): Strategy C likely wins — captures most efficiency gains while partially containing the tax avoidance externality. A wins if you discount tax avoidance risk. B wins only if legal access equity is weighted heavily.
Under Rawlsian welfare (maximize worst-off): Strategy B wins — public defenders serve the most disadvantaged. C second. A worst (rich get richer).
Under libertarian framework: A wins — free market, voluntary transactions, minimal interference. B wins under effective altruism (maximize lives improved per dollar).

The meta-answer: no single strategy dominates across all value systems. The question reveals that technology deployment decisions are fundamentally political/ethical choices that cannot be resolved by technical analysis alone. The right answer identifies this rather than picking one definitively.

question: You are the head of a space agency with a $10B budget for the next decade. You must choose between: (A) Moon base (cost $8B, 60% success probability, scientific value 7/10, economic value 3/10, timeline risk: medium), (B) Mars mission (cost $12B — requires international partnership bringing $4B, 35% success, scientific 10/10, economic 2/10, high timeline risk), (C) Asteroid mining demo (cost $3B, 75% success, scientific 4/10, economic 9/10, low timeline risk), (D) Space telescope array (cost $4B, 85% success, scientific 9/10, economic 1/10, low risk). You can combine options within budget, but B requires a partnership you don't yet have. Design a portfolio strategy that maximizes expected value across both scientific and economic dimensions, accounts for the partnership risk in B, and hedges against program failure. Show your work.
rubric: Budget constraint: $10B. B requires $12B total but only $8B from agency with $4B partnership — agency pays $8B still. So B costs agency $8B if partnership secured.

Partnership risk for B: If partnership fails (say 40% chance), B can't proceed. Expected agency cost of pursuing B: 60% chance partnership secures ($8B spent) + 40% chance fails ($0-1B in sunk planning costs).
Portfolio options within $10B: C+D=$7B, leaves $3B unallocated. A=$8B, leaves $2B. A+C=$11B — over. C+D+partial=feasible.
Expected scientific value (EV_sci) per mission: A=0.6*7=4.2. B=0.35*10=3.5. C=0.75*4=3.0. D=0.85*9=7.65.
D dominates on scientific EV per dollar ($4B for 7.65). C dominates on economic EV per dollar ($3B for 6.75). A is mediocre on both. B has high scientific ceiling but low EV and partnership risk.
Optimal portfolio: C+D=$7B, EV_sci=10.65, EV_econ=7.60. Remaining $3B: pursue B partnership negotiation risk-free (sunk cost minimal), or add A partially.

Hedge strategy: C+D provides reliable economic and scientific returns. Use remaining $3B as partnership development fund for B — if partnership materializes, cancel D and do A+B (but this requires sequencing decision early). Best hedged portfolio: commit to C+D, invest $2B in partnership development for future B, hold $1B reserve.

Key insight: D is the highest-EV scientific mission and lowest risk — it should be in every portfolio. C is the highest-EV economic mission. The question is whether to gamble on A or B with remaining capital.

question: Four political parties are forming a coalition government. Party A has 35 seats, B has 28, C has 22, D has 15. Total seats: 100. A majority requires 51 seats. Any subset of parties reaching 51 is a viable coalition. Each party has policy preferences (1-10 scale): on taxation (A=2,B=5,C=7,D=9), on immigration (A=8,B=6,C=4,D=2). Coalition negotiations involve compromising on policy. Parties will only join a coalition if they get policy outcomes closer to their ideal than staying in opposition. Using Shapley values, calculate each party's bargaining power. Then show which coalitions are stable (no party can defect and do better), and predict the likely governing coalition.
rubric: Viable coalitions (≥51 seats): AB=63, AC=57, AD=50 (no), BC=50 (no), BD=43 (no), CD=37 (no), ABC=85, ABD=78, ACD=72, BCD=65, ABCD=100. Viable: AB, AC, ABC, ABD, ACD, BCD.
Wait: AD=50 fails. BC=50 fails. BD=43 fails. CD=37 fails. Valid minimal winning coalitions: AB(63), AC(57), BCD(65).
Shapley value measures marginal contribution to all coalitions. Party A appears in almost every minimal coalition — high Shapley value. D appears in only BCD — lower value.

Shapley values (approximate): A has veto power since no coalition without A reaches 51 except BCD. B and C together with D can exclude A. So A doesn't have complete veto. Shapley: A≈0.333, B≈0.25, C≈0.25, D≈0.167 (approximate symmetric game).

Policy stability: AB coalition — tax compromise at ~3.5, immigration ~7. C and D in opposition. AC coalition — tax ~4.5, immigration ~6. B in opposition. BCD — tax ~6.5, immigration ~3.5. A in opposition.

Stable coalition analysis: AB is stable if A and B prefer each other over alternatives. But C could offer A a better immigration deal (4 vs B's 5 — A prefers lower immigration so actually B=6 is closer to A=8 than C=4). A prefers B on immigration. On tax, A prefers B(5) over C(7).
Predicted coalition: AB — A gets better policy outcomes with B than C or D on both dimensions. AB is also minimum winning (no incentive to add C or D and dilute cabinet positions).

BCD can form but only if B, C, D all prefer their coalition policy outcome to being in AB or AC respectively. B in BCD gets tax=6.5 (worse than B's ideal 5, but B in AB also gets ~3.5 — actually B prefers AB for tax policy too). BCD is unlikely stable for B.

question: An autonomous vehicle company must decide its liability and safety strategy. Two approaches: (A) Deploy conservatively — vehicles only operate in geo-fenced easy conditions, 99.999% safety rate, market coverage 30% of scenarios. (B) Deploy aggressively — operate in all conditions, 99.97% safety rate, full market coverage. With 10 million vehicles on the road each driving 15,000 miles/year, calculate expected annual deaths under each approach. Factor in: human drivers cause 1.3 deaths per 100M miles. Then model the game-theoretic interaction: if your competitor chooses B and you choose A, you capture only 30% of their market and fall 3 years behind. How should safety standards be set, and by whom, to avoid a race to the bottom?
rubric: Total miles driven: 10M vehicles * 15,000 mi = 150 billion miles/year.
Human baseline: 1.3 deaths per 100M miles * 1,500 = 1,950 deaths/year from human driving (same mileage).

Strategy A: 99.999% safety = 0.001% failure rate per... need to convert. 99.999% safety over what unit? Assume per mile: 0.00001 failures/mile. But this is ambiguous. More likely: failure rate meaning death per mile. If 99.999% safe over 100M miles = 1 death per 100M miles. Deaths: 150B miles * 1/100M = 1,500 deaths/year. Better than human (1,950). But only 30% coverage — so 30% of miles are AV (45B miles), 70% still human (105B miles). AV deaths: 45B * 1/100M = 450. Human deaths on remaining 70%: 105B * 1.3/100M = 1,365. Total: 1,815.
Strategy B: 99.97% safety = 3 deaths per 100M miles. Full coverage. Deaths: 150B * 3/100M = 4,500. Worse than all-human baseline of 1,950!

Critical finding: Strategy B's aggressive deployment actually INCREASES deaths vs. the all-human baseline despite AVs being 'safer than humans' in the 99.97% framing — because the denominator matters.
Game theory: if competitor B deploys and you A, they capture 70% market. But both companies choosing B creates 4,500 deaths/year — worse than the human baseline. Classic race to the bottom.

Regulatory solution: set a mandatory minimum AV safety standard of at least human-equivalent (≤1.3 deaths per 100M miles). Any company below this standard cannot deploy. This prevents competitive pressure from degrading safety below human baseline. The standard should be set by regulators (NHTSA), not companies, because companies face prisoner's dilemma incentives.

question: A startup wants to disrupt a market dominated by a single incumbent with 80% market share. The incumbent has $5B in cash, 10x your team size, brand dominance, and existing customer relationships. Your startup has: a genuinely superior product (3x better on the key metric), $50M Series B, and a 2-year window before the incumbent copies your core innovation. Design a complete go-to-market strategy that maximizes the probability of surviving and winning, using game-theoretic reasoning to predict and counter the incumbent's likely responses at each stage.
rubric: Identifies the core strategic problem: the incumbent WILL copy in 2 years. The startup must either (a) win enough market to be defensible, or (b) get acquired, or (c) build a moat that can't be copied.

Phase 1 (months 0-6) — Beachhead strategy: don't attack the incumbent's core market. Find a customer segment the incumbent ignores or serves poorly (e.g., SMBs, specific vertical, new geography). Establish dominance there first. The incumbent has no incentive to defend a small segment — they'll ignore you.

Phase 2 (months 6-18) — Flywheel: use beachhead segment to generate case studies, data moats (your product improves with more users), and talent. Expand to adjacent segments. The 3x superior product creates word-of-mouth that's hard to buy.

Phase 3 (months 18-24) — Force the incumbent's hand: by the time the incumbent copies, you have 2 years of data, brand in niche, customer lock-in (switching costs), and possibly network effects. The copy won't be as good initially.

Incumbent's likely responses and counters: (1) Price war — counter by serving segments where price isn't the primary decision. (2) Free tier or copying features — counter by moving up-market (premium positioning). (3) Acquisition offer — be prepared to negotiate or use as leverage for Series C at higher valuation. (4) FUD (Fear, Uncertainty, Doubt) — counter with transparent benchmarks and case studies.

Game-theoretic prediction: if you reach $20M ARR and significant customer base in 2 years, the incumbent's optimal move is acquisition (cheaper than competing). This is actually the desired outcome for most VCs. Set the target: be worth $200-400M to the incumbent.
Key risk: the window is 2 years, not 5. Speed of execution is the primary resource. Don't optimize for profitability — optimize for defensible market position. Use all $50M offensively.

Moat construction: identify what the incumbent structurally cannot copy even if they try — regulatory approvals, customer data relationships, specialized talent, API integrations that create switching costs.

question: Design the optimal tournament structure for a 64-team competition where: (1) the best team should win with highest probability, (2) every game should matter (no lame duck matches), (3) the tournament must complete in 3 weekends (6 days total, max 8 games per day), (4) fans prefer seeing top teams play as late as possible (for drama). Compare single elimination, double elimination, Swiss system, and a round-robin top-8 hybrid. Calculate the probability that the true best team wins under each format, and design the format that best balances all four constraints.
rubric: Single elimination: 63 games, 6 rounds. Fits in 6 days easily (max ~11 games/day). Best team wins with probability ~50% (each game is ~50/50 in theory, but if best team wins each game with p=0.7, P(win 6 games)=0.7^6≈0.12). Every game matters. Top teams may meet early (seeding helps but not guarantees). Easy to schedule. Probability best team wins: 50-60% with good seeding.
Double elimination: 127 games max. Cannot fit in 6 days at 8 games/day (48 total game slots). Fails the time constraint.

Swiss system (7 rounds): 64 teams, 7 rounds = 224 games over 7 rounds. But only 32 games/round (64/2). 7 rounds at 8 games/day parallel = feasible if games are simultaneous. Produces clear winner without elimination drama. Every game matters. Top teams meet late naturally. Best team wins probability: ~75-80%. Fits if simultaneous games allowed.
Round-robin top-8 hybrid: Round 1: 8 groups of 8, round-robin (28 games each = 224 games total — WAY too many).

Optimal design: Modified Swiss + Playoff. Days 1-4: 5 rounds of Swiss with 64 teams (32 simultaneous games/day = fine). Top 8 advance. Days 5-6: single elimination bracket with top 8 (7 games). Seeding from Swiss record. Result: best team likely reaches top 8 (high Swiss probability), then wins 3 more games (0.7^3=0.34 for win given top 8). Combined: P(best wins)≈0.80*0.34=0.27... actually Swiss top-8 qualification for true best team is ~95%. Then P(win 3-game bracket) = 0.7^3 ≈ 0.34. Total: ~32%.
Best probability of true winner: Swiss 7 rounds (75-80%) >> Swiss+playoff hybrid (~32%) >> single elimination (~12-20%). But Swiss lacks drama of knockout.

The four constraints create genuine tension: high winner-probability (Swiss) conflicts with drama/elimination format (single elim). Optimal compromise: Swiss 5 rounds + top-16 double elimination bracket in final 2 days.

question: A country must choose between three economic development strategies over 20 years. Strategy A (export-led growth, East Asian model): requires suppressing domestic consumption (savings rate 40%), investment in manufacturing infrastructure ($500B), produces 7% annual GDP growth for 15 years then slows to 3%. Strategy B (consumption-led growth): requires less investment ($100B), produces 4% growth for 20 years, higher income equality. Strategy C (innovation-led growth): requires $800B in R&D and education, low growth (2%) for first 10 years, then 9% for years 11-20. Starting GDP: $1T. Calculate terminal GDP for each strategy after 20 years, the year each strategy produces the highest cumulative GDP, and design a hybrid that captures the best of all three.
rubric: Strategy B: Years 1-20 at 4%: GDP = $1T * 1.04^20 = $2.19T.
Terminal GDP ranking: A ($3.20T) > C ($2.89T) > B ($2.19T).
Cumulative GDP comparison: A produces high GDP early (15 years of fast growth). C produces low GDP for first 10 years but catches up. B is steady but lower. A likely has highest cumulative GDP too.
Year of crossover: C overtakes B around year 14-15 when innovation returns kick in. C approaches A by year 18-19.

Hybrid strategy: A for first 10 years (export-led manufacturing, build infrastructure), transition to C in years 10-20 (use manufacturing surplus to fund R&D/education). Expected outcome: 7% for 10 years then 6-9% innovation-led. GDP at year 10: $1T*1.07^10=$1.97T. Year 20: $1.97T*1.08^10=$4.25T — potentially exceeds all pure strategies.

Real-world consideration: Strategy A requires political suppression of consumption (authoritarian context often needed), Strategy C requires educational/institutional capacity that takes time to build. Hybrid faces transition risk (switching costs when moving from manufacturing to innovation economy).

question: A military commander has 100 units distributed among 5 outposts (A=30, B=25, C=20, D=15, E=10). An adversary with 80 units will attack exactly 2 outposts simultaneously. You can redistribute your forces before the attack, but moving units costs time — each unit moved reduces fighting effectiveness by 5% per outpost crossed (adjacent outposts are: A-B, B-C, C-D, D-E). You win an outpost if your defending units > attacking units. Losing both attacked outposts costs 100 points; losing one costs 40 points; losing none costs 0. The adversary knows your starting positions but not your redistribution. Model the optimal redistribution as a minimax problem and find your optimal mixed strategy.
rubric: Identifies this as a minimax problem: you choose redistribution, adversary chooses attack target knowing your post-redistribution positions (but not your mixed strategy).
Adversary's optimal attack: concentrate 80 units on 2 outposts to guarantee both wins. If you have any outpost with <40 units (half of 80), adversary can take it and the adjacent outpost.

Current distribution: if adversary attacks A(30)+B(25)=55, they win both easily (80 > either individually). You need at least 41 units at each defended position to hold against 40 concentrated attackers.
Redistribution options: concentrate 80 units at 2 positions (keep 20 at others). E.g., move to A=40, B=40, abandon C=10, D=5, E=5. But movement costs apply.
Movement cost: moving 10 units from C to B (adjacent) reduces those 10 units' effectiveness by 5% = effectively 9.5 units. Minimal movement is optimal.

Minimax pure strategy: distribute 40-40 at two positions, accept loss of 3 positions. But adversary attacks the other two of your 40-unit outposts if you reveal which two you're defending. Need mixed strategy.

Mixed strategy: randomize which two outposts to defend heavily. With 5 outposts and 2 to defend, there are C(5,2)=10 possible defense pairs. Choose uniformly = each pair with 10% probability. Adversary cannot guarantee winning both targets.

Expected cost of minimax mixed strategy: with 10 equally likely defense pairs and adversary attacking optimally, they win both only when they hit your two undefended positions — probability depends on strategy. This is Colonel Blotto variant requiring formal analysis.

Practical answer: concentrate 40 units each at A and C (non-adjacent, maximizes coverage), accept E and D as abandoned, contest B. Mixed strategy: randomly concentrate at {A,C}, {A,D}, {B,D} with weights maximizing adversary's minimum success probability.

question: Three tech giants (A, B, C) are each deciding whether to adopt a new AI safety protocol that costs $500M to implement and generates $0 direct revenue, but reduces industry-wide AI accident probability from 5% to 1% per year. An accident causes $100B in damages split across the industry (proportional to market share: A=50%, B=30%, C=20%). The protocol only works if all three adopt it (network effect). Model this as a coordination game: find all Nash equilibria. Then add: B has private information that their own internal AI has a 15% accident probability regardless of the protocol. How does this hidden type change the equilibrium? What mechanism could induce honest revelation?
rubric: Baseline game: each company's expected damage from accident without protocol: A=$2.5B (50%*$5B annual expected), B=$1.5B, C=$1B. With all adopting protocol: A=$500M cost + $0.5B expected damage savings ≈ saves A $2B net annually but only if all cooperate.

More precisely: without protocol, annual expected accident cost: 5% * $100B * share. A=5%*50B=$2.5B. B=5%*30B=$1.5B. C=5%*20B=$1B. With all adopting: 1%: A=$0.5B, B=$0.3B, C=$0.2B. Savings: A=$2B, B=$1.2B, C=$0.8B. Cost: $500M each. Net benefit: A=$1.5B, B=$0.7B, C=$0.3B.
Nash equilibria analysis: If all others adopt, does C adopt? Benefit=$0.3B < cost=$0.5B. C defects! C's dominant strategy is not to adopt. Protocol fails because C won't join.
But if C doesn't adopt, protocol doesn't work — A and B also won't adopt. Equilibrium: none adopt.

Alternative equilibrium: all adopt (if mechanism forces or side payments exist). This is a coordination game with multiple equilibria: all-adopt (efficient but unstable due to C) and none-adopt (inefficient).

B's hidden type: B has 15% internal accident probability, meaning even with the protocol B generates 15% accident risk, making the protocol's effectiveness 0 for B's accidents. B's rational strategy: free-ride — appear to adopt but don't invest fully. This hidden type means the protocol doesn't achieve 1% industry-wide even if nominally adopted.

Mechanism for honest revelation: (1) Independent auditing with financial penalties for non-compliance. (2) Insurance markets — companies that truly adopt get lower premiums, revealing types through pricing. (3) Mandatory incident disclosure laws — B's 15% accident rate would be observable over time. (4) Regulator-designed Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) mechanism where companies reveal true accident probabilities and pay/receive accordingly — truth-telling is dominant strategy.

question: You are the CEO of a dominant search engine facing a disruptive AI competitor that has launched a conversational search product with 20M users (growing 50% month-over-month). Your search engine has 4B users, $50B annual revenue, and 15,000 engineers. The AI competitor burns $500M/month and has 24 months of runway. You have three options: (A) Acquire the competitor ($20B price, 6-month regulatory review, 40% deal failure risk), (B) Launch a competing product using internal AI (estimated 18 months to match quality, $2B investment), (C) Partner with the competitor (revenue share, distribution for their product, no acquisition). Model the 5-year outcome of each strategy including the competitor's likely counter-moves, the regulatory landscape, and user switching dynamics. Which strategy maximizes your probability of maintaining >50% search market share in year 5?
rubric: Baseline threat: 50% MoM growth from 20M users. If sustained for 24 months: 20M*1.5^24 ≈ 1.4B users. This is catastrophic for search market share. But 50% MoM is unsustainable — realistic growth slows to 5-10%/month after 12 months. Even so, competitor reaches 200-400M users in 2 years.

Option A (Acquire): Cost $20B. 40% failure risk = $0 outcome from that 40% scenario (competitor remains independent). Success (60%): you own the technology. But 6-month review window means competitor grows uncontested. Competitor may also take funding from others during review to raise walk-away price. After acquisition, integration risk (talent exodus). Expected value: 60%*(maintain market leadership) + 40%*(back to competing independently, now 6 months behind). Regulatory: DOJ/FTC likely to challenge given your dominance. Risk of blocking is higher than 40% in current antitrust environment.

Option B (Build): 18-month lag is dangerous. Competitor grows from 20M to ~200M during that period. But you have distribution (4B users, existing search traffic). Once launched, you can redirect users. $2B is cheap relative to $20B acquisition. Key risk: internal product quality may not match competitor in 18 months — you need external AI capabilities. Counter-move: competitor raises more money at inflated valuation once they see you're building.

Option C (Partner): Revenue share gives competitor distribution they lack. They grow faster but you control the relationship. You can discontinue the partnership once you've built your own product. Competitor knows this — they may decline or demand equity instead of revenue share. Partnership buys time but doesn't eliminate the threat.

Competitive dynamics: at $500M/month burn and 24-month runway, competitor must raise or exit by month 24. If you don't acquire, their Series D/E at ~200M users could value them at $30-50B — acquisition becomes impossible. Window is 12 months max to acquire at reasonable price.

5-year market share analysis: A succeeds (60%) → likely >50% share. A fails (40%) → must pursue B, already 6 months behind = risk of dropping to 40-45% share. B alone → 35-50% share depending on execution. C → share drops to 55-60% short term but stabilizes if partnership maintained. Pure probability of >50% share in year 5: A≈60%*90%=54%. B≈50%. C≈55%.

Recommended strategy: B+C hybrid — immediately launch partnership (C) to buy time and learn from competitor's technology, while aggressively building internal product (B), and maintain acquisition optionality if regulatory environment clears. Don't bet exclusively on acquisition.

question: A logistics company must route 5 delivery trucks to serve 20 customers. Truck capacity: 10 deliveries each. Customer delivery windows are 2 hours wide and start at: 8am(3 customers), 10am(5 customers), 12pm(4 customers), 2pm(5 customers), 4pm(3 customers). Deliveries take 20 minutes each. A truck takes 30 minutes to travel between time windows. Can all customers be served with 5 trucks? If not, what is the minimum number of trucks needed?
rubric: Each truck can serve consecutive windows if travel time allows: 8am window ends ~10am, travel 30min, arrive 10am — just fits second window if last delivery in 8am window is at 9:40am.
Per time window capacity needed: 8am=3, 10am=5, 12pm=4, 2pm=5, 4pm=3. Peak concurrent need is 5 trucks (at 10am and 2pm windows).

With 5 trucks: assign 5 trucks to the 10am window (can each do 1 delivery), then rotate. But 5 deliveries in 10am window * 20 min = all done by 11:40am. Travel 30 min → arrive 12:10pm — late for 12pm window start but within 2-hour window (ends 2pm). Feasible.

A truck serving 8am window (3 customers, 3 trucks needed) has 2 trucks idle. Those 2 idle trucks can start 10am window. 3 8am-window trucks finish by 9am, travel 30min, arrive 9:30am — too early for 10am window but can wait. All 5 trucks available for 10am.

Key constraint: 10 deliveries per truck. A truck doing 8am(1 delivery), 10am(1), 12pm(1), 2pm(1), 4pm(1) = 5 deliveries — well within 10 capacity. 5 trucks * 5 windows = 25 delivery slots. Need 20 deliveries. Feasible with 5 trucks.
Answer: Yes, 5 trucks can serve all 20 customers. The binding constraint is the peak window (5 customers at one time), requiring exactly 5 trucks.

question: A city is choosing between two transit expansions: (A) Extend the subway 3 miles to a dense neighborhood (cost: $800M, serves 50,000 daily riders, opens in 4 years). (B) Build bus rapid transit on 5 corridors (cost: $200M, serves 80,000 daily riders total, opens in 1 year). Compare on: cost per daily rider, time to benefit, 20-year ridership (assume 3% annual growth for subway, 1% for BRT due to traffic congestion), and flexibility if ridership projections are wrong. Which investment is better?
rubric: Cost per rider: Subway = $800M/50K = $16,000. BRT = $200M/80K = $2,500. BRT is 6.4x cheaper per rider.

Time to benefit: Subway opens in 4 years (36 months of BRT already delivering during that gap). BRT advantage in years 1-4 is enormous: 80K riders * 365 * 3 years = 87.6M extra rider-days delivered before subway even opens.
20-year ridership (from year 1): BRT: 80K * sum(1.01^t, t=0..19) = 80K * 22.0 = 1.76M rider-years. Subway starts year 4: 50K * sum(1.03^t, t=0..15) = 50K * 19.6 = 980K rider-years total.
BRT delivers 79% more total rider-service over 20 years despite lower growth rate (due to 3-year head start and higher initial ridership).
Flexibility: BRT routes can be adjusted if neighborhood patterns change. Subway is a permanent infrastructure commitment. BRT wins on optionality.

Subway advantages: higher quality (grade-separated, immune to traffic), likely induces more economic development, higher growth rate, potentially more riders in year 20 (50K*1.03^20=90K vs 80K*1.01^20=98K — BRT still slightly ahead at year 20).

Recommendation: BRT is the better investment on virtually every metric (cost, speed, total ridership, flexibility). Subway only wins if underground infrastructure enables >2x the ridership growth rate long-term and you heavily discount short-term benefits.

question: A startup founder is offered a job at a big tech company with $300K total comp. Their startup has a 20% chance of a $10M exit and 80% chance of $0, over 3 years. The founder owns 60% of the startup. They earn $80K/year at the startup currently. Compare the expected 3-year financial outcome of staying vs. leaving. What non-financial factors should the founder weight, and what is the 'risk premium' they're implicitly accepting by staying?
rubric: Stay at startup: 3-year income = 3*$80K=$240K. Expected exit value = 20%*($10M*60%)=$1.2M. Total expected = $240K + $1.2M = $1.44M.
Take big tech job: 3-year income = 3*$300K=$900K. No startup equity. Total = $900K.
Expected value of staying ($1.44M) > leaving ($900K) by $540K. But staying has high variance (80% chance you earn only $240K vs. $900K guaranteed).

Risk premium: the founder accepts a $660K lower guaranteed income ($900K - $240K) in exchange for the 20% shot at $1.2M. The actuarially fair premium is $1.44M - $900K = $540K expected upside. A risk-averse founder might not accept this.
Non-financial factors: autonomy, mission alignment, learning velocity, cap table ownership feelings, what you'd regret at age 70, team relationships.

Key insight: if the founder needs the $300K income (has dependents, debt), the 80% scenario of earning only $240K over 3 years is materially harmful. Risk tolerance is income-level dependent — the same bet means very different things to people with different financial cushions.

question: A streaming service has 10M subscribers at $15/month. Churn is 5%/month. They're considering a price increase to $18/month. Market research suggests: at $18, churn rises to 7%/month but no new subscription price-related benefits. However, new subscriber growth (currently 400K/month) drops to 300K/month. Model the subscriber count and monthly revenue for both scenarios over 12 months. At what month does the price-increase scenario produce lower total revenue, if ever?
rubric: Current scenario ($15): Each month, subscribers = prev*(1-0.05) + 400K. Converges to 400K/0.05 = 8M (but starts at 10M, so declining to equilibrium). Month 1: 10M*0.95+400K=9.9M. Revenue=$148.5M. Month 2: 9.9*0.95+0.4=9.805M. R=$147M. Trend: declining toward 8M.
Price increase ($18): Each month = prev*(1-0.07) + 300K. Equilibrium = 300K/0.07=4.28M. Month 1: 10M*0.93+300K=9.6M. Revenue=$172.8M. Month 2: 9.6*0.93+0.3=9.228M. R=$166M. Declining faster.

Revenue comparison month by month: M1: $18=$172.8M vs $15=$148.5M. $18 wins. M6: $18≈10M*(0.93^6)+(300K*sum of 0.93^k). After 6 months starting at 10M with monthly additions... approximately 10M*0.66+300K*4.0=6.6M+1.2M=7.8M. Revenue=7.8*18=$140.4M. $15 scenario: 10M*0.74+400K*3.7=7.4M+1.48M=8.88M. Revenue=$133.2M. $18 still ahead at month 6.

Month 12 comparison: $18 scenario likely still ahead in revenue per subscriber (18/15 = 20% premium) despite lower count. The revenue crossover requires the $15 scenario to have significantly more subscribers — specifically 20% more. The rate at which $15 gains on $18 depends on differential churn (2pp) and acquisition (100K/month).

Conclusion: the $18 price likely never produces LOWER monthly revenue than $15 over 12 months, because the 20% revenue-per-subscriber advantage is too large for the modest acquisition/churn differences to overcome in that timeframe. The crossover (if any) occurs after year 1.

question: A charity has $1M to allocate across three programs. Each program has diminishing returns described by: Program 1 (malaria nets): QALYs = 500*sqrt(dollars/1000). Program 2 (deworming): QALYs = 200*ln(dollars/100 + 1). Program 3 (vitamin supplements): QALYs = 0.0001*dollars. You must spend at least $100K on each program. How should you allocate the remaining $700K to maximize total QALYs? (You may use calculus or numeric approximation.)
rubric: At $100K: P1 marginal = 0.25/sqrt(100)=0.025 QALYs/$. P2 marginal = 2/(1000+1)≈0.002 QALYs/$. P3 = 0.0001 QALYs/$.

P1 has by far the highest marginal return. Allocate additional $700K primarily to P1. P1 marginal drops as x increases: at $800K, P1 marginal = 0.25/sqrt(800)=0.0088. P2 marginal at $100K still 0.002. Keep adding to P1 until marginal equals P2's marginal.
Equimarginal condition: 0.25/sqrt(x1/1000) = 2/(x2/100+1) where x1+x2=800K (remaining after minimums).
Numeric: P1 at $700K: marginal=0.25/sqrt(700)=0.00945. P2 at $100K: marginal=0.002. P1 still dominant. P1 at $800K: 0.25/sqrt(800)=0.00884. P3 always 0.0001 — never optimal beyond minimum.
Optimal: put all $700K additional into P1. Final: P1=$800K, P2=$100K, P3=$100K. Total QALYs: P1=500*sqrt(800)=14,142. P2=1,382. P3=10. Total≈15,534 QALYs.

question: A company is planning to enter three new markets over 5 years. Market A (North America): $10B TAM, 24-month sales cycle, requires regulatory approval (18 months, 70% success probability). Market B (Europe): $6B TAM, 18-month sales cycle, requires GDPR compliance build (12 months, 95% success). Market C (Asia-Pacific): $4B TAM, 12-month sales cycle, requires local partnership (6 months to find partner, 60% success). You have capacity to pursue all three in parallel but only $50M in expansion budget. Estimate the expected revenue by year 5 from each market given start times, success probabilities, and 2% market capture rate. Which sequence maximizes expected year-5 revenue?
rubric: Market A: 18-month regulatory + 24-month sales cycle = 42 months before first revenue. Year 5 = 60 months. Active revenue period: 60-42=18 months. Revenue if successful: $10B*2%*(18/12)=$300M. P(success)=70%. Expected=$210M.
Market B: 12-month compliance + 18-month sales cycle = 30 months. Active: 30 months. Revenue: $6B*2%*(30/12)=$300M. P(success)=95%. Expected=$285M.
Market C: 6-month partnership + 12-month sales cycle = 18 months. Active: 42 months. Revenue: $4B*2%*(42/12)=$280M. P(success)=60%. Expected=$168M.
All parallel expected year-5 revenue: $210M+$285M+$168M=$663M. But $50M budget may constrain parallel pursuit.
Budget allocation: if each market requires equal investment and $50M is split 3 ways ($16.7M each), parallel pursuit is feasible. If any single market requires more than $25M, sequencing is needed.

Optimal sequence if must serialize: B first (highest expected revenue, second-fastest timeline), then C (fastest path to revenue, start 12 months in), then A (slowest, start parallel with B if budget allows).

Key insight: market B has the highest expected revenue per dollar and second-fastest timeline. C starts generating revenue earliest but has lowest probability. A has highest ceiling but slowest path. Parallel pursuit of B+C with deferred A is likely optimal if budget constrains true parallelism.

question: Two pharmaceutical companies are racing to develop competing drugs for the same disease. Company A is 12 months ahead. Both would capture 100% of the market if they're first ($2B NPV), or 30% of market if second ($600M NPV). Each company can choose to accelerate (spend $200M extra, halves remaining development time) or maintain pace. Company A has 24 months remaining (or 12 if accelerating). Company B has 36 months (or 18 if accelerating). Analyze the game tree, find the Nash equilibrium, and explain whether Company B should accelerate.
rubric: Without acceleration: A finishes month 24, B finishes month 36. A wins easily ($2B NPV). B gets $600M NPV.
B accelerates, A doesn't: A finishes 24, B finishes 18. B WINS. B gets $2B-$200M=$1.8B. A gets $600M.
A accelerates, B doesn't: A finishes 12, B finishes 36. A still wins. A gets $2B-$200M=$1.8B. B gets $600M.
Both accelerate: A finishes 12, B finishes 18. A wins. A gets $1.8B. B gets $600M-$200M=$400M.

B's dominant strategy analysis: If A doesn't accelerate: B accelerates → $1.8B vs not accelerate → $600M. B accelerates. If A accelerates: B accelerates → $400M vs not → $600M. B does NOT accelerate when A accelerates.

A's analysis: If B accelerates, A must accelerate (A does: wins $1.8B; A doesn't: B wins, A gets $600M). If B doesn't accelerate, A can accelerate (A does: $1.8B; A doesn't: $2B). A should NOT accelerate if B doesn't accelerate.

Nash equilibrium: B accelerates, A doesn't → B wins. But A anticipates this and accelerates to prevent B from winning. If A accelerates: B's best response is NOT to accelerate (save $200M, still get $600M). This suggests: A accelerates, B doesn't = Nash equilibrium. A gets $1.8B, B gets $600M.

B's strategic conclusion: B should accelerate ONLY if A doesn't accelerate (which A won't rationally choose given B's threat). Since A will rationally respond to any B acceleration by accelerating themselves, B's threat to accelerate isn't credible as a sustained strategy. B should save the $200M.

question: A city is experiencing a housing crisis. Average rent has increased 60% in 5 years. Three policy options: (A) Rent control (caps at current rent, grandfathered units, new construction exempt), (B) Zoning liberalization (allow 3x more units to be built, takes 5 years to have effect), (C) Public housing construction ($500M for 10,000 units, 3-year timeline). Model the 10-year impact on rent levels, housing supply, and inequality. Identify the second-order effects that make each policy worse than it appears, and design a hybrid policy.
rubric: Rent control second-order effects: (1) Reduces supply — landlords convert to condos or let properties deteriorate. (2) Misallocation — tenants stay in too-large units to keep low rent. (3) Dual market — controlled units become commodities (key money, illegal subletting). (4) Discourages new construction in exempt new units if market signals are dampened. Long-term: may worsen housing supply despite short-term relief.

Zoning liberalization second-order effects: (1) 5-year lag means no near-term relief. (2) Filtering takes years — new construction is luxury, affordable housing comes 10-20 years later as luxury units age. (3) Requires infrastructure investment (schools, transit) that may not follow. (4) NIMBYism may prevent full implementation. (5) May increase land values before supply expands, temporarily worsening affordability.

Public housing second-order effects: (1) Stigma and concentration effects — concentrated poverty in public housing. (2) Requires ongoing operating subsidies. (3) 10,000 units is small vs. typically 100K+ unit shortage. (4) Government is a worse manager than private market in most cases. (5) Crowds out private investment.

Hybrid policy: (1) Means-tested rental vouchers (portable subsidies, not tied to specific units — avoid misallocation of rent control). (2) Expedited permitting for affordable housing construction (by-right approval). (3) Inclusionary zoning (require 15-20% affordable in all new developments). (4) Land value tax to encourage development and fund affordable housing without distorting rental market. This combination addresses supply and demand sides simultaneously.

10-year rent trajectory: A alone = rents don't fall but supply shrinks, eventually prices surge when units exit market. B alone = rents fall 15-25% by year 10 as supply clears. C alone = marginal impact (1-2% of market). Hybrid = 10-15% rent reduction by year 10, more equitable distribution.

question: A hedge fund manager must decide whether to hold or sell a position. The stock is at $100. The fund's model gives: 40% probability of $150 in 6 months, 35% probability of $100 (unchanged), 25% probability of $60. The fund has a mandate not to lose more than 15% in any 6-month window (they're at exactly -14.8% YTD). A $60 outcome would violate the mandate. Analyze the decision using: (1) pure expected value, (2) mandate-constrained expected value, (3) Kelly criterion. What does each framework prescribe, and which should the manager use?
rubric: Pure EV: E[price] = 0.4*150 + 0.35*100 + 0.25*60 = 60+35+15=$110. Expected return = 10%. EV says hold.

Mandate-constrained EV: If $60 outcome occurs, mandate breached → fund faces redemptions, manager may be fired. Assign catastrophic cost to $60 outcome. If job loss = -$5M career value, then: 0.4*($50 gain) + 0.35*$0 + 0.25*(-$40 loss - $5M career cost) ≈ $20-$0-$1.26M. Sell to avoid the 25% tail risk.

Kelly criterion: Kelly fraction f = (p*b - q) / b where b = gain/loss ratio. Multiple outcomes: for simplified analysis, treat as binary between EV=$110 and current $100: f = edge/odds = (0.10)/1.0 = 10% of wealth. Manager should only hold 10% of the position, implying partial sell.
Framework recommendations: EV = hold all. Mandate = sell all. Kelly = sell 90%, hold 10%.

Which to use: The mandate is a hard constraint — violating it has career and fund consequences that aren't captured in pure EV. A professional fund manager operating under a mandate must treat the mandate as a binding constraint, not a soft preference. The correct framework is mandate-constrained optimization, which says sell enough of the position to guarantee the -15% threshold isn't breached even in the worst case.
Practical answer: sell enough shares to ensure that even at $60, the portfolio doesn't breach -15%. This is a risk-management calculation, not an alpha calculation.

question: A country has 30% of its population (60M people) in food insecurity. Available interventions: (A) Cash transfers — $50/person/month, reaches 80% of food insecure, reduces food insecurity by 70% among recipients. (B) Food subsidies — reduces staple food prices 30%, reaches 100% of food insecure, reduces insecurity by 40%. (C) Agricultural investment — $2B one-time cost, increases domestic food production 25% over 5 years, reduces insecurity by 50% in year 5. Annual budget for this initiative: $3B. Which single intervention maximizes year-1 food security improvement? Which combination maximizes year-5 improvement within budget?
rubric: Year-1 analysis: 60M food insecure people. A: reaches 80%=48M, reduces 70% of those: 48M*0.7=33.6M people helped. Annual cost: 48M * $50 * 12 = $28.8B — WAY over $3B budget. Recalculate: $3B/($50*12)=5M people max. A reaches 5M, helps 3.5M. B: $3B for subsidies reaching all 60M, reduces insecurity 40%: 24M people helped. C: year-1 impact minimal (5-year program). B wins year-1 clearly.

Year-5 analysis: B annual cost assumed ~$1B/year (subsidy administration); A can scale. If $1B/year on A: reaches $1B/($50*12)=1.67M people, helps 1.17M. If $1B on B: helps 24M (40% of 60M). If $2B on C (one-time), helps 50% of remaining insecure in year 5.

Combination: Year 1: $3B on B (helps 24M). Years 2-4: $2.5B/year on B + $0.5B savings toward C. By year 3: $1.5B saved for C investment. Year 4: deploy $2B C investment. Year 5: B (24M helped) + C (additional 50% of remaining insecure). Total year-5: ~36M helped.

Alternative: $1B on C upfront + $2B on B in year 1 — but C costs $2B so can only do one. Commit $2B to C in year 1 and $1B to B: helps 8M (B only) in year 1. Year 5: C kicks in for full 50% benefit + continue B.
Best year-5 strategy: invest $2B in C + $1B in B in year 1. Years 2-5: $3B/year on B. Year 5: B (24M) + C (additional 18M from production increase) = 42M helped out of 60M insecure.

question: An AI company is choosing between three compute strategies: (A) Own hardware — $200M upfront, $20M/year operating, gives 100% control, lasts 5 years before needing refresh. (B) Cloud rent — no upfront, $60M/year, flexible scaling. (C) Hybrid — $80M upfront for core capacity, $25M/year cloud for peak demand. Assume 15% annual growth in compute needs requires increasing cloud spend proportionally. Calculate 5-year TCO for each strategy. Then add: there's a 30% chance that a breakthrough reduces compute needs by 50% in year 3. How does this change the analysis?
rubric: Strategy A: $200M upfront + 5*$20M = $300M. Fixed capacity — growth beyond capacity requires cloud anyway, but assume A covers initial needs.
Strategy B: Year 1=$60M. With 15% growth: Y2=$69M, Y3=$79.4M, Y4=$91.3M, Y5=$105M. Total = $404.7M.

Strategy C: $80M upfront + Year 1=$25M cloud. Cloud grows 15%/year. Total cloud: 25*(1+1.15+1.15^2+1.15^3+1.15^4)=25*6.74=$168.5M. Fixed=$80M+5*$0 operating. Wait, C has $80M upfront + cloud at $25M/year growing. Total=$80M+$168.5M=$248.5M.
5-year TCO ranking: C ($248.5M) < A ($300M) < B ($404.7M). Hybrid wins.

With 30% chance of 50% compute reduction in year 3: A: No benefit — already paid $200M+ regardless. Savings minimal (lower opex). B: 70% normal path ($404.7M) + 30% reduced path: Y1=$60M, Y2=$69M, Y3=$39.7M, Y4=$45.6M, Y5=$52.5M = $266.8M. Expected B = 0.7*404.7+0.3*266.8 = $283.3+$80=$363.3M. C: 0.7*248.5 + 0.3*(80+25+28.75+19.85+22.8+26.2=80+122.6=$202.6M) = $173.9+$60.8=$234.7M.

With breakthrough risk: C still wins. B's flexibility partially captures breakthrough value. A's fixed costs are sunk regardless. Hybrid (C) provides the best balance of upfront commitment and scalable flexibility.

question: A social network has a cold-start problem in a new country. Launching with 0 users, the network effect means: value per user = k * (number of users)^0.6. Users join if value > $5 (their opportunity cost). k = 0.5. What is the minimum number of users needed to be self-sustaining (new users want to join)? How do you bootstrap to that number? Design a launch strategy that accounts for the multi-sided coordination problem.
rubric: Self-sustaining threshold: 0.5 * N^0.6 = 5. N^0.6 = 10. N = 10^(1/0.6) = 10^1.667 ≈ 46 users.
So only 46 users are needed before the network becomes self-sustaining — surprisingly low. This means the cold-start problem is solvable by seeding a small community.

Bootstrap strategy: (1) Seed with committed early adopters — recruit 50-100 influencers or community leaders who create content even before others join. (2) Temporary subsidies — offer users a bonus (gift cards, premium features) to join the first 1,000, reducing their opportunity cost below $5. (3) Closed beta for specific community (e.g., university campus) where network effects concentrate geographically before national rollout.

Multi-sided coordination problem: users join for content, creators join for audience, advertisers join for users. Classic chicken-and-egg. Solution: subsidize the side that's harder to attract first. On a social network, engaging creators must come before passive users.

Launch sequence: (1) Pre-launch: recruit 200 content creators with guaranteed minimum earnings/visibility. (2) Invite-only beta with waitlist (creates scarcity/FOMO). (3) Seed with existing communities (Facebook groups, Reddit threads, Discord servers) to migrate cohesive groups rather than individuals — each group migration adds cluster of users instantly, exceeding the 46-user threshold within the group.

Anti-network-effect risks: existing incumbents with much higher user counts. Differentiation must be strong enough that value = 0.5*N^0.6 grows faster for the niche the startup targets vs. general-purpose incumbents.

question: A military must allocate defense budget across five threat domains: cyber ($500M needed), air ($800M), maritime ($600M), land ($400M), space ($300M). Total need: $2.6B. Available budget: $2B. Each domain has a minimum viable capability threshold — funding below the threshold provides 0 defense value. Thresholds: cyber=$200M, air=$500M, maritime=$400M, land=$200M, space=$150M. Above threshold, value increases linearly. Given the budget, which domains should be funded to threshold and which above? What is the optimal allocation assuming equal weight to all threat domains?
rubric: Total minimum thresholds: $200+$500+$400+$200+$150=$1,450M. Remaining after minimums: $2,000-$1,450=$550M.
All domains can be funded to minimum (within budget). Now allocate $550M above minimums.
Maximum additional investment per domain (cap = needed - threshold): Cyber=$300M, Air=$300M, Maritime=$200M, Land=$200M, Space=$150M.

With equal weights and linear returns above threshold, the optimal strategy is to equalize the marginal value across domains. Since all are linear and equally weighted, distribute $550M proportionally to remaining capacity.
Total remaining capacity: $300+$300+$200+$200+$150=$1,150M. $550M/$1,150M=47.8% of remaining capacity funded.

Alternative: if threats have different probabilities, prioritize by threat*value. Cyber attacks are highest-frequency — could justify over-weighting. Space is emerging but low current threat — could fund to threshold only. This is the key strategic judgment layer beyond the math.

question: A country implements carbon pricing at $50/ton CO2. Neighboring countries have no carbon price. Domestic manufacturers face higher costs: steel industry cost increases 15%, cement 20%, aluminum 25%. Manufacturers can: (a) absorb costs (reduce profits), (b) pass costs to consumers (reduce demand), (c) relocate to neighboring countries (carbon leakage). Model each industry's likely response using profit-maximizing logic. What policies prevent carbon leakage without abandoning the carbon price? Calculate the carbon border adjustment needed to equalize competitive pressure.
rubric: Steel (15% cost increase): if demand is elastic, can't pass costs without losing sales. If international competitors don't have the cost, they undercut domestic producers. Likely response: mix of absorption + relocation threat. In trade-exposed industries, leakage is high.
Cement (20% increase): cement is heavy and expensive to ship — less trade-exposed. Domestic producers can pass more costs to consumers without leakage risk. Likely response: cost pass-through.
Aluminum (25% increase): aluminum is electricity-intensive and globally traded. High leakage risk — smelters relocate to countries with cheap coal power. Most vulnerable to leakage.
Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM): import tariff on foreign goods equivalent to the carbon price they avoided. Calculation: if steel uses X tons CO2 per ton of steel, import tariff = X * $50.
For steel: ~1.8 tons CO2/ton steel. CBAM = 1.8 * $50 = $90/ton steel border adjustment. This equalizes the competitive cost between domestic (pays carbon tax) and foreign (pays CBAM) producers.

Policy design: (1) CBAM for import-competing industries. (2) Free allowances for export-oriented industries (EU ETS approach). (3) Revenue from CBAM funds domestic industry adaptation. (4) International carbon clubs — negotiate reciprocal carbon prices with major trading partners to eliminate CBAM need.
Trade law consideration: CBAM must be WTO-compliant — tied to actual embedded carbon content, not discriminatory, available to all countries equally.

question: A deep-sea oil company has a $500M drilling opportunity. Success (25% probability) yields $3B in oil revenue. Failure (75%) yields $0. Before drilling, they can buy seismic data for $20M. The seismic data has 85% sensitivity (correctly identifies success) and 75% specificity (correctly rules out failure). Using Bayesian analysis, determine: (1) the probability of striking oil given positive seismic results, (2) the expected value of the seismic data, (3) the maximum price they should pay for the data.
rubric: Prior: P(oil)=0.25, P(no oil)=0.75.
P(positive seismic | oil) = 0.85. P(positive seismic | no oil) = 1 - specificity = 0.25.

Decision tree with seismic: If positive (prob 0.40): drill. EV = 0.531*$3B - $500M = $1,593M - $500M = $1,093M. If negative (prob 0.60): don't drill. EV = $0. Expected EV with seismic: 0.40*$1,093M + 0.60*$0 - $20M = $437.2M - $20M = $417.2M.
Decision without seismic: EV = 0.25*$3B - $500M = $750M - $500M = $250M.

Value of seismic information: $417.2M - $250M = $167.2M. Maximum price for seismic = $20M + $167.2M = $187.2M (the $20M paid is sunk, so max additional = $167.2M, meaning total willingness to pay is $167.2M + currently paying $20M → they should pay up to $167.2M total for the data, so the $20M price is well below the information's value).

question: You are the chief strategist of a country with GDP $500B, 50M population, and a dominant export sector (40% of GDP) in a commodity that AI is making progressively less valuable — the commodity's real price has fallen 30% in 5 years and is projected to fall another 50% over the next decade. Design a 20-year economic transformation strategy that transitions the country away from commodity dependence while maintaining political stability (current unemployment 8%, poverty rate 22%). The strategy must be politically implementable (specify which constituencies win and lose at each stage), economically sound, and robust to three shock scenarios: global recession, regional conflict, and climate disruption.
rubric: Diagnoses the core problem: 40% of GDP from a commodity losing 50% of value = 20% GDP erosion if no action. This is a structural transformation challenge, not a cyclical one.

Phase 1 (Years 1-5) — Build the foundation while the commodity still earns: Use commodity rents to fund education (technical training for commodity workers), infrastructure (digital connectivity, ports), and institutional capacity (rule of law, contract enforcement). Political buy-in: current commodity workers get retraining, government credibly commits to their transition. Winners: educated workers, cities. Losers: commodity company owners facing higher taxes.

Phase 2 (Years 5-12) — Diversification push: Use foundation to attract manufacturing FDI (wage arbitrage), build a domestic services sector, and develop tourism/agriculture as alternative exports. Political buy-in: new jobs absorb commodity workforce. Key risk: premature deindustrialization if wages rise before productivity does.
Phase 3 (Years 12-20) — Knowledge economy: Technology sector, financial services, high-value-added manufacturing. This requires Phase 1 and 2 to have succeeded.

Political implementation: Create a sovereign wealth fund from commodity revenues NOW (before prices fall further) to fund transition. Guarantee commodity workers a 10-year income floor — this co-opts the most politically powerful opposition. Stagger reforms to ensure there's always a growing sector absorbing labor.

Shock robustness: Global recession — commodity-dependent economies get hit hard; the diversification hedge is exactly the protection needed. Regional conflict — ensures domestic food/energy security in Phase 1. Climate disruption — agriculture-based alternative exports may be threatened; prioritize climate-resilient crops and digital services (climate-proof).

The answer that is incomplete: one that proposes transformation without addressing the political economy (who bears the transition costs and why they'd accept it) fails the 'politically implementable' criterion.

question: A pandemic response team has 3 months and $2B to allocate across five simultaneous interventions for a novel respiratory virus spreading at R=2.5. Each intervention has a cost, implementation time, and R-reduction effect: (A) Mask mandates ($100M, 2 weeks, R reduction 0.3), (B) Ventilation improvement ($500M, 6 weeks, R reduction 0.4), (C) Rapid testing infrastructure ($300M, 4 weeks, R reduction 0.5), (D) Vaccine accelerated development ($800M, 12 weeks — just outside window, R reduction 0.8 afterward), (E) Hospital capacity expansion ($400M, 8 weeks, prevents R from rising due to overwhelmed care, value = prevents R from increasing 0.3 when hospitals overwhelmed). Current trajectory: hospitals overwhelmed by week 6. Allocate the $2B to minimize cumulative infections over 3 months.
rubric: Problem framing: need to get R below 1.0 (currently 2.5) before hospitals overwhelm, and prevent overflow. Without intervention, R=2.5 leads to exponential growth.
E is critical: without E, hospitals overwhelm at week 6, causing R to rise by 0.3 (to 2.8 effectively). E costs $400M. Highly recommended.
Within 3 months (12 weeks): D doesn't deliver within window. D is poor investment for this specific 3-month objective.
Best R-reducing interventions within 12 weeks: A (week 2, -0.3), C (week 4, -0.5), B (week 6, -0.4).
Cumulative R reduction: deploy A+C+B+E = $100+$300+$500+$400=$1.3B within budget. Remaining $700M: D has no 3-month impact — save or invest in C/A scale-up.

R trajectory: Week 2: 2.5-0.3=2.2. Week 4: 2.2-0.5=1.7. Week 6: 1.7-0.4=1.3 AND E prevents overflow (+0.3 prevented) so effective R=1.3 not 1.6. Week 6 R=1.3 still above 1.0 but dramatically reduced. Continue all interventions.

Need R<1: add more scale of existing interventions with remaining $700M. Additional mask mandates (multiple campaigns: $100M each, additional 0.15 per campaign), additional testing ($300M → 0.3 more). By week 8-10 could reach R~0.9-1.0.
Optimal allocation: A+C+B+E ($1.3B) + reinvest $700M in scaling A+C for additional R reduction. Cumulative infections minimized by getting R<1 by week 8.

question: A dominant platform (70% market share) is deciding how to respond to a new open-source competitor that is gaining 5% market share per quarter. The platform has three strategic options: (A) Embrace and extend — adopt the open standard, add proprietary extensions, then make extensions mandatory (classic Embrace-Extend-Extinguish). (B) Compete on quality — invest $5B in product improvement to outperform the open alternative. (C) Form an open standards body — co-opt the open-source movement by controlling the standards process. Model the multi-period game where open-source developers are a third player who can choose: cooperate with platform (A or C), maintain independence, or accelerate development. Find the equilibria and explain which strategy is most likely to succeed and why.
rubric: Open-source developers as third player: their incentives are anti-monopoly (they started the open-source project to reduce platform dependence). Any platform strategy that smells like co-optation will be resisted.

Strategy A (EEE): Short-term — platform gains open-source users. Long-term — developers detect the Extend-Extinguish phase and fork the project (Linux vs Unix, LibreOffice vs OpenOffice). EEE has failed repeatedly in modern markets because developers are sophisticated. Equilibrium: developers resist, fork, platform loses trust permanently.

Strategy B (compete on quality): Platform invests $5B, improves product. Developers continue open-source in parallel. Users may prefer platform if quality gap is large enough. This is sustainable if platform can maintain quality lead. But $5B is one-time; open-source compounds for free. Equilibrium: quality parity in 5-7 years, platform share stabilizes at 50-60%.

Strategy C (standards body): Platform joins or creates a standards body. If done transparently (true governance), developers may participate. If done to control, developers detect and exit. Success probability depends on credibility of governance commitment. Historical examples: Java (Sun/Oracle failed when Oracle asserted control), IETF (government/academic success). Equilibrium: if platform credibly commits, open-source developers join, standard becomes dominant, platform captures implementation advantage.

Most likely to succeed: B (compete on quality) is the most reliable — it doesn't require co-opting an adversarial community. The platform already has distribution, data advantages, and $5B is affordable. C could work if genuinely independent governance is possible. A is the highest-variance strategy — either total capture or total loss of developer trust.

Key game-theoretic insight: the platform's credibility problem. Any strategy that requires developers to trust the platform's long-term intentions is vulnerable to rational suspicion of future defection. Compete-on-quality (B) is the only strategy that doesn't require trust from the open-source community.

question: A global humanitarian organization must evacuate 50,000 refugees from three conflict zones over 90 days. Zone A: 20,000 refugees, accessible but 200km from safe zone. Zone B: 15,000 refugees, partially blocked (50% of routes), 350km. Zone C: 15,000 refugees, actively contested, 500km, can only be accessed during 6-hour windows every 48 hours. Fleet: 50 buses (40 seats each), 10 trucks (80 seats). Each vehicle can make 2 trips/day maximum. Design a 90-day evacuation plan that minimizes casualties (priority: Zone C first due to active conflict, then B, then A). Calculate if the plan is feasible, and identify the binding constraint.
rubric: Daily capacity: 50 buses * 40 seats + 10 trucks * 80 seats = 2,000 + 800 = 2,800 seats per trip. 2 trips/day = 5,600 people/day maximum.
90-day total capacity: 5,600 * 90 = 504,000 — far exceeds 50,000. The bottleneck is NOT total capacity.

Zone C constraint: 6-hour windows every 48 hours = 15 windows in 90 days. Each window: can mobilize full fleet for 1 trip (5,600 capacity per trip). 15 windows: 15 * 5,600 = 84,000 capacity — more than enough for 15,000 Zone C refugees. Zone C feasible.
Zone B constraint: 50% route blockage halves effective fleet or doubles time. Effectively 2,800 people/day from Zone B. 15,000 / 2,800 = 5.4 days. Feasible.
Zone A: 20,000 / 5,600/day = 3.6 days. Very feasible.
Sequencing: Days 1-30: Focus on Zone C (using 48-hour windows, 15 trips). Also run Zone B in parallel when not in Zone C window. Days 30-60: Complete Zone B. Days 60-90: Complete Zone A.

Binding constraint: Zone C's 48-hour access window limits the pace of that evacuation specifically, but 15,000 people in 15 available windows (1,000 people per window) is feasible with the fleet. The true binding constraint is coordination and logistics (driver fatigue, fuel, vehicle maintenance on 200-500km routes), not raw capacity.

Casualties risk: Zone C active conflict means delay costs lives. Prioritize Zone C windows even at cost of slower Zone A/B progress. Recommendation: pre-position vehicles near Zone C before each window to maximize window utilization.

question: The EU is designing an AI regulation framework. Three proposals: (A) Precautionary ban on high-risk AI (medical, criminal justice, hiring) until proven safe — blocks ~40% of current AI applications. (B) Mandatory certification with 12-month review process — 40% of AI companies can't survive 12 months without revenue. (C) Liability framework — AI companies are financially liable for harms; no pre-approval needed. Analyze the second and third-order effects of each proposal on: innovation rate, safety outcomes, competitive dynamics (US/China comparison), and democratic legitimacy. Then design a framework that achieves 90% of the safety benefits of A with 10% of the innovation cost.
rubric: Proposal A second-order effects: Innovation: EU AI sector shrinks, talent migrates to US/UK. Safety: High in blocked domains, but innovation goes underground (no regulatory oversight of offshore tools used by EU citizens). Competitive: EU falls behind in regulated domains that US/China capture. Legitimacy: Initially popular but backlash when medical AI breakthroughs happen elsewhere.

Proposal B second-order effects: 40% startup mortality means only well-funded companies can navigate certification. Creates oligopoly (large incumbents can afford 12-month review). Safety: certification becomes checkbox exercise rather than genuine evaluation. Innovation: chill effect on entire sector, not just high-risk applications. Competitive: disadvantages EU startups vs US (no certification) and China (government-backed, no survival risk).

Proposal C second-order effects: Innovation: maximum. Safety: liability only works if harm is detectable and attributable — many AI harms are diffuse (algorithmic discrimination, privacy erosion) and hard to litigate. Large companies can self-insure against liability. Small companies go bankrupt on first major incident. Competitive: most similar to US approach.

Optimal hybrid framework: (1) Tiered risk classification — only truly high-risk applications (criminal justice, medical diagnosis) require certification; medium-risk get post-market surveillance. (2) Fast-track certification (30 days) for startups with bond/insurance requirement instead of full review. (3) Sandboxes — regulated testing environments where companies can deploy with immunity while gathering safety data. (4) Strict liability for demonstrable harms (not pre-approval), with mandatory liability insurance creating market-based safety incentives. (5) Standards-based (not rules-based) certification — publish safety standards, let industry certify compliance, audit ex-post.
This captures: A's safety in truly high-risk domains, C's innovation freedom in low-risk domains, B's accountability without the survival-destroying timelines.

question: You are a founding engineer at a company that just received a $2B acquisition offer. You have 0.5% equity. Current company value (409A): $500M. The acquisition offer is $2B (4x the 409A). You have the right to exercise 100,000 options at $10/strike (current 409A price: $50/share). Two scenarios: (A) Accept acquisition — exercise options at $10, sell at $2B-valuation price immediately. (B) Reject acquisition — company aims for IPO in 3 years with 60% probability of $5B valuation, 40% probability of $800M. Calculate your expected financial outcome under each path, accounting for taxes (assume federal + state = 45% on ordinary income, 20% on long-term capital gains if you've held >1 year), and make the optimal decision.
rubric: Current equity: 0.5% of company. At $2B acquisition: 0.5% * $2B = $10M.

Option exercise: 100,000 options at $10 strike. At $2B/$500M = 4x current 409A price. If $2B total, shares at $2B / total_shares. With 0.5% equity: $10M proceeds from equity. But option exercise gain = ($50*4 - $10) * 100K = ($200-$10)*100K = $190*100K = $19M gain. Tax: if exercised and immediately sold, ordinary income tax applies (45%): net = $19M * 0.55 = $10.45M. Plus no additional equity calculation needed — $10M from existing vested equity. Total acquisition: $10M (equity) + $10.45M (options) = $20.45M.

IPO path (reject): 0.5% equity + options not yet exercised. 60% chance: $5B IPO. Equity = 0.5%*$5B = $25M. Options = ($5B/$500M*$50 - $10)*100K = ($500-$10)*100K = $490*100K = $49M — but with dilution at IPO (say 20% dilution) ≈ $39.2M. If held 1+ year post-IPO: long-term capital gains at 20%. Equity net: $25M*0.8=$20M. Options net: $39.2M*0.8=$31.36M. Total: $51.36M. 40% chance: $800M IPO. Equity = 0.5%*$800M=$4M. Options = ($800M/$500M*$50-$10)*100K ≈ ($80-$10)*100K=$7M. Long-term: equity=$3.2M, options=$5.6M. Total=$8.8M.
Acquisition: $20.45M. IPO expected: $34.34M. On pure EV, reject the acquisition.

But: acquisition is certain, IPO is 3 years away with binary outcomes. Risk-adjusted: a moderate risk-averse person may prefer $20M certain over $34M expected with 40% chance of only $8.8M. The key question is whether you can afford the $8.8M downside scenario — if you have dependents, debt, or no other savings, $20M guaranteed may be the right choice despite lower EV.

question: A pharmaceutical company has discovered a molecule that cures a disease affecting 500,000 people globally — 450,000 in low-income countries (LIC) who can't pay more than $100/treatment, 50,000 in high-income countries (HIC) who can pay up to $50,000/treatment. Development cost: $2B. Manufacturing cost: $200/treatment. Patent life: 20 years. Design a global pricing strategy that: (1) recovers development costs, (2) maximizes patients treated globally, (3) satisfies shareholders (15% ROI minimum), and (4) is defensible against compulsory licensing by LIC governments. Show all calculations.
rubric: Revenue from HIC at $50,000: 50,000 patients * $50,000 = $2.5B. Manufacturing cost: 50,000 * $200 = $10M. HIC contribution to fixed cost recovery: $2.5B - $10M = $2.49B. This recovers the $2B development cost + leaves $490M.
Shareholders: need 15% ROI on $2B investment = $300M profit minimum over patent life. HIC alone generates $490M above costs — shareholder requirement met from HIC alone.

LIC pricing: since development costs recovered by HIC, the optimal LIC price is just above manufacturing cost = $200-250/treatment. But LIC patients can only pay $100. Consider: cross-subsidize LIC at $100 (lose $100/patient in manufacturing costs, covered by HIC surplus). 450,000 * $100 = $45M revenue - 450,000 * $200 = $90M cost. LIC loss = -$45M. HIC surplus ($490M) covers LIC loss ($45M) with $445M remaining. Total profit: $445M > $300M minimum. Shareholder requirement still met.
So: charge HIC $50,000, charge LIC $100 (at manufacturing loss), recover everything from HIC pricing. ALL 500,000 patients treated globally.

Compulsory licensing defense: if LIC governments issue compulsory licenses (forcing generic production), company loses LIC market but keeps HIC. LIC revenue anyway = only $45M vs $90M manufacturing cost — company is better off if LIC goes generic (saves $45M loss). Counter-intuitively, allowing LIC generic licensing IMPROVES company finances while helping patients.

Optimal strategy: proactively partner with generic manufacturers for LIC supply (medicine patent pool), charge $100 in LIC, $50,000 in HIC. This prevents compulsory licensing fights, generates positive PR, fully satisfies shareholders, and treats all patients.

question: Two superpowers (A and B) are in an arms race. Each year, each independently decides to invest in conventional weapons (C), nuclear weapons (N), or diplomacy (D). Payoff structure is complex: C vs C = arms race drain (-2, -2). N vs C = intimidation advantage (+3, -4). C vs N = (-4, +3). N vs N = mutual destruction risk (-10, -10) with probability 5%, else (-3, -3). D vs anything = (-1, +1) if other plays C or N; (0,0) if both play D. This game repeats indefinitely. Model the Nash equilibria for one-shot and repeated games. Under what conditions can 'both diplomacy' be sustained as an equilibrium? What external mechanism could shift the equilibrium from the arms race to mutual disarmament?
rubric: One-shot analysis: N dominates C for A (if B plays C: N gives +3 > C gives -2). If both play N: expected payoff = 0.95*(-3) + 0.05*(-10) = -2.85-0.5 = -3.35. Playing D gives -1 if other plays N. So D > N when other plays N (-1 > -3.35). A's dominant strategy analysis is complex — no pure dominant strategy exists.
N vs N yields -3.35 expected. C vs C yields -2. C vs C is better than N vs N! Both would prefer mutual conventional arms.
But: if A plays C, B has incentive to play N (+3 vs -2). So C is not safe. Mixed strategy Nash: each plays N with positive probability to deter the other from playing N.

Diplomacy equilibrium: D vs D = (0,0). This Pareto dominates all other outcomes. But D is exploitable — if A plays D and B plays N, B gets +1 (relatively). So D is not a Nash equilibrium in one-shot (too exploitable).

Repeated game: D vs D can be sustained via grim trigger if discount factor is high enough. Deviation from D: get +1 once, then trigger retaliatory N vs N = -3.35/period. Cooperate: 0/(1-δ). Deviate: +1 + δ*(-3.35)/(1-δ). Cooperate if 0 >= 1 + δ*(-3.35-0)/(1-δ). → 0 >= 1 - 3.35δ/(1-δ). → 3.35δ/(1-δ) >= 1. → 3.35δ >= 1-δ. → 4.35δ >= 1. → δ >= 0.23. Diplomacy is sustainable with very low discount rate requirement!

External mechanism: arms control treaties with verification (IAEA/START model), mutual reduction sequencing with third-party verification, economic integration making war costly, nuclear taboo norms. The repeated game already sustains diplomacy at δ>0.23 — the main need is coordination on which equilibrium to play and a monitoring mechanism to detect deviations early.

question: A sovereign wealth fund manages $500B. The fund must decide asset allocation for the next decade under four scenarios: (A) Tech boom — equities +15%/yr, bonds -2%/yr, real assets +5%/yr, probability 25%. (B) Stagflation — equities -5%/yr, bonds -3%/yr, real assets +8%/yr, probability 30%. (C) Deflationary recession — equities -15%/yr, bonds +6%/yr, real assets -4%/yr, probability 20%. (D) Balanced growth — equities +8%/yr, bonds +3%/yr, real assets +4%/yr, probability 25%. The fund has a mandate to at least preserve real purchasing power (0% real return) in every scenario and to maximize expected 10-year return. Find the allocation (equities/bonds/real assets summing to 100%) that satisfies the mandate constraint while maximizing expected return, using a mean-variance or scenario-based approach.
rubric: Sets up the problem as: max E[return] = 0.25*R_A + 0.30*R_B + 0.20*R_C + 0.25*R_D subject to: R_B >= 0 (stagflation), R_C >= 0 (recession), R_A >= 0, R_D >= 0.
Portfolio return in each scenario: R = w_e*r_e + w_b*r_b + w_r*r_r where w sum to 1. Scenario B (hardest constraint): -5%*w_e - 3%*w_b + 8%*w_r >= 0. Scenario C: -15%*w_e + 6%*w_b - 4%*w_r >= 0.
From scenario C: -15w_e + 6w_b - 4w_r >= 0. With w_e + w_b + w_r = 1, substitute w_r = 1-w_e-w_b: -15w_e + 6w_b - 4(1-w_e-w_b) >= 0 → -11w_e + 10w_b >= 4.
Expected return per unit is highest for real assets (3.85%), then equities (1.25%), then bonds (0.55%). Maximize real assets subject to constraints.

Solution: maximize w_r. Given constraints, find corner solution. From constraint system: approximately w_r ≈ 0.50-0.60, w_b ≈ 0.25-0.30, w_e ≈ 0.10-0.20. Final answer: approximately 50% real assets, 30% bonds, 20% equities — satisfying both hard scenarios while maximizing expected return.

question: You are advising a researcher who has made a discovery that could either: (a) be published immediately for maximum scientific credit, risking scoop by better-resourced labs who will see the preprint and move faster, (b) be patented first (6 months delay), protecting commercial applications but limiting academic credit, or (c) be shared selectively with 3 trusted collaborators to strengthen the paper, adding 12 months but dramatically improving quality and defensibility. The discovery is in a competitive field where 4 other groups are working on similar problems, each approximately 6-18 months behind. The researcher is junior (pre-tenure) and needs both academic credit and commercial applications for career and funding. Model the strategic landscape: under what conditions is each option optimal? What is the mixed strategy that maximizes expected career outcome, and how does the optimal strategy change if one of the competitors is 6 months behind vs. 18 months behind?
rubric: Maps the decision tree: (a) publish now — get credit if no one scoops, lose credit if scooped. (b) patent first — delays publishing by 6 months, potentially enough for a well-funded competitor 6 months behind to scoop the academic paper. (c) collaborate — strongest paper but 12-month delay, very risky if any competitor is <12 months behind.

Competitor analysis: if closest competitor is 18 months behind, all three options are feasible without scoop risk. If closest is 6 months, option (c) is suicidal (12 months gives them time to publish first), and option (b) is risky (6 months patent delay means they might scoop the paper before patent is even filed).

Option (a) optimal conditions: Closest competitor >6 months away, preprint establishes priority before scoop is possible, patent protection isn't critical (academic/nonprofit work), pre-tenure urgency.

Option (b) optimal conditions: Commercial applications are high-value, closest competitor >8 months away (6 month patent + 2 month buffer), researcher's institution has strong tech transfer support, career not solely dependent on academic citation metrics.
Option (c) optimal conditions: Closest competitor >15 months away, collaborators are fast and trusted, field rewards deep quality over speed, researcher is tenured or has long horizon.

Mixed strategy: File a provisional patent immediately (1-page, $300, establishes date) preserving option (b) without the 6-month delay. Post preprint within 2 weeks (establishes priority, option a benefits). Simultaneously invite collaborators to post-preprint review (option c benefits from quality improvement without delay cost). The provisional patent + preprint combination is the dominant mixed strategy — it achieves 90% of the benefits of each option.

Competitor 6 months vs 18 months away: at 6 months, don't collaborate (c is out), file provisional + publish immediately. At 18 months, full collaboration is viable. The key strategic insight: the provisional patent + preprint filing is robust across both scenarios and dominates pure strategies in competitive fields.
